questions.prompt: Stripe webhook handler. Why enqueue for processing instead of doing the work inline?

choices.text: Stripe (and most webhook providers) requires fast 200 response — typically within ~10 seconds — otherwise considers the delivery failed and retries (often aggressively). Doing your actual work inline (updating database, sending notifications, syncing CRM) can exceed this window, causing Stripe to retry your webhook, causing duplicate processing. Right pattern: verify the signature synchronously (fast, must happen before ack), enqueue the actual work, ACK to Stripe immediately. The consumer Worker processes the queued message asynchronously — your actual work has all the time it needs without affecting webhook delivery. Same pattern for GitHub, Twilio, any high-volume webhook source.

questions.explanation: Webhook providers require fast 200 response; enqueue for async processing while ACKing immediately.

questions.prompt: Queue consumer processes payments. Same payment occasionally processed twice. Cause?

choices.text: At-least-once delivery semantics. Cloudflare Queues (like SQS, most queues) guarantee delivery but may deliver the same message twice in rare cases (transient failures, retry mid-processing). Consumer must be IDEMPOTENT — running twice produces the same final state as running once. Patterns: (1) Check before action — 'has this payment ID already been processed?' Look up by external ID; skip if found. (2) Use database constraints — UNIQUE on payment ID makes second insert fail safely. (3) Use natural idempotency — upserts, set-equal operations. For payments specifically, never trust the queue alone for exactly-once; build idempotency into the consumer. Standard pattern across all queue systems.

questions.explanation: At-least-once delivery; make consumers idempotent (check before action, unique constraints, upserts).
